GCE A LEVEL MARKING SCHEME

 SUMMER 2024 (Predicted)

 A LEVEL
 ECONOMICS – COMPONENT 3
 A520U30-1H – Hard

 About this marking scheme
 The purpose of this marking scheme is to provide teachers, learners, and other interested parties,
 with an understanding of the assessment criteria used to assess this specific assessment.

 This marking scheme reflects the criteria by which this assessment was marked in a live series and
 was finalised following detailed discussion at an examiners' conference. A team of qualified
 examiners were trained specifically in the application of this marking scheme. The aim of the
 conference was to ensure that the marking scheme was interpreted and applied in the same way
 by all examiners. It may not be possible, or appropriate, to capture every variation that a candidate
 may present in their responses within this marking scheme. However, during the training
 conference, examiners were guided in using their professional judgement to credit alternative valid
 responses as instructed by the document, and through reviewing exemplar responses.

 Without the benefit of participation in the examiners' conference, teachers, learners and other
 users, may have different views on certain matters of detail or interpretation. Therefore, it is
 strongly recommended that this marking scheme is used alongside other guidance, such as
 published exemplar materials or Guidance for Teaching. This marking scheme is final and will not
 be changed, unless in the event that a clear error is identified, as it reflects the criteria used to
 assess candidate responses during the live series.
 _________________________________________________________________________


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                         1
 GENERAL MARKING GUIDANCE

 Positive Marking
 It should be remembered that learners are writing under examination conditions and credit should
 be given for what the learner writes, rather than adopting the approach of penalising him/her for
 any omissions. It should be possible for a very good response to achieve full marks and a very
 poor one to achieve zero marks. Marks should not be deducted for a less than perfect answer if it
 satisfies the criteria of the mark scheme, nor should marks be added as a consolation where they
 are not merited.

 For each question there is a list of indicative content which suggest the range of economic
 concepts, theory, issues and arguments which might be included in learners' answers. This is not
 intended to be exhaustive and learners do not have to include all the indicative content to reach the
 highest level of the mark scheme.

 The level-based mark schemes sub-divide the total mark to allocate to individual assessment
 objectives. These are shown in bands in the mark scheme. For each assessment objective a
 descriptor will indicate the different skills and qualities at the appropriate level. Learner's responses
 to questions are assessed against the relevant individual assessment objectives and they may
 achieve different bands within a single question. A mark will be awarded for each assessment
 objective targeted in the question and then totalled to give an overall mark for the question.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                             2
 Section A.
 1. Major supermarkets accused of "greedflation" during the cost-of-living crisis
 1 1 Explain what is meant by price elasticity of demand and the main factors affecting it. [10]

                                    AO1                                                   AO3
Band                              6 marks                                               4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                               3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                                     Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of PED, including formula,          Strong chains of reasoning linking each
           coefficients and classifications (elastic, inelastic,   determinant (substitutes, necessity, proportion of
           unitary, perfectly elastic/inelastic).                  income, time, habit) to the size of PED.
2          3-4 marks                                               1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                          Limited analysis
           A narrower range of factors or superficial              Chains of reasoning are generally not well
           treatment.                                              developed.
1          1-2 marks                                               1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                       Limited analysis
           Only a basic knowledge.                                 Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                                 0 marks
           No relevant knowledge.                                  No valid analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 Price elasticity of demand (PED) measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change
 in price, calculated as %∆Qd / %∆P.
 Values: |PED| > 1 elastic; |PED| < 1 inelastic; |PED| = 1 unitary; 0 perfectly inelastic; ∞ perfectly
 elastic.
 PED is typically negative due to the law of demand.

 AO3 (factors affecting PED)
 Substitutes: more close substitutes → more elastic demand (e.g. Coca-Cola vs supermarket
 own-brand). Few or no substitutes → inelastic (e.g. petrol, cigarettes).
 Necessity vs luxury: necessities (e.g. bread, prescription medicines) are inelastic; luxuries (e.g.
 designer handbags, holidays) are more elastic.
 Proportion of income: low-cost items (chewing gum, matches) are inelastic as price changes are
 trivial; big-ticket items are more elastic.
 Time: in the short run, demand tends to be more inelastic because consumers cannot easily
 switch; in the long run it becomes more elastic (e.g. petrol – switch to EVs).
 Addiction/habit: addictive goods (alcohol, tobacco, gambling) tend to be inelastic.
 Brand loyalty: strong brands have lower PED (Apple).
 Breadth of definition: narrow definitions (Fuji apple) are elastic; broad definitions (fruit) are
 inelastic.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                        3
 1 2 Discuss whether firms with significant pricing power always act to exploit consumers. [20]

                       AO1                                  AO3                                    AO4
Band                 6 marks                             6 marks                              8 marks
3        5-6 marks                           5-6 marks                            6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge                 Excellent analysis                   Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of pricing      Clear reasoning on how firms         Balanced two-sided answer that
         power (monopoly, oligopoly,         may or may not exploit their         considers different market
         monopolistic competition) and its   position, including profit           structures, regulation and
         relationship to consumer welfare.   maximisation, satisficing, ethical   stakeholder theory. Reasoned
                                             and strategic considerations.        judgment.
2        3-4 marks                           3-4 marks                            3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                      Good analysis                        Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                     Good argument line.                  Strong two-sided answer.
1        1-2 marks                           1-2 marks                            1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                   Limited analysis                     Limited evaluation
         Few points.                         Superficial.                         Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                             0 marks                              0 marks
         No knowledge.                       No analysis.                         No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (For exploitation)
 Profit-maximising firms with pricing power set P > MC, extracting consumer surplus and creating
 allocative inefficiency.
 Supermarkets have faced allegations of "greedflation" – expanding margins during the
 cost-of-living crisis and the CMA launched formal investigations into grocery pricing.
 Price discrimination (first, second, third degree) can transfer consumer surplus to firms.
 Dynamic pricing and algorithmic pricing allow firms to extract surplus from consumers at peak
 demand (Uber surge pricing, airlines).
 Limit pricing and predatory pricing can entrench market power and harm long-run competition.
 Inelastic demand (necessities) combined with market power is particularly concerning – energy,
 fuel, pharmaceuticals, essential foods.

 AO1/AO4 (Against / qualifications)
 Firms may not actually profit maximise – Baumol (sales), Williamson (managerial), behavioural
 theories of the firm suggest satisficing behaviour.
 Competitive pressures from domestic rivals and imports constrain pricing power even with a high
 market share – the theory of contestable markets.
 Reputation and long-run profit considerations discipline firms – exploiting consumers today
 damages repeat purchases and brand value.
 Stakeholder theory / CSR: firms balance consumer, employee, supplier, community interests
 against shareholder returns.
 Regulation (CMA, Ofgem, Ofwat, FCA) constrains exploitation through price caps, structural
 remedies and behavioural undertakings.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                   4
 Dynamic efficiency: supernormal profits fund R&D;, producing new products and lower costs that
 eventually benefit consumers (e.g. pharmaceutical innovation).
 Some price discrimination benefits consumers – e.g. lower fares for students/pensioners,
 expanding access.

 Qualifiers
 Depends on market structure and the degree of contestability.
 Depends on regulatory effectiveness.
 Depends on firm objectives (short vs long run, profit vs satisficing).
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                  5
 2. Streaming wars intensify: Netflix loses subscribers as Disney+ and Apple TV+ expand
 2 1 Outline what is meant by a contestable market and explain the conditions required for
 contestability. [10]

                                     AO1                                               AO3
Band                                6 marks                                          4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                           3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                                 Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of contestable markets theory   Strong chains linking the conditions (low entry/exit
           (Baumol), including hit-and-run entry and sunk      barriers, low sunk costs, good information, equal
           costs.                                              access to technology) to the disciplining effect on
                                                               incumbents.
2          3-4 marks                                           1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                      Limited analysis
           Definition understood but conditions superficial.   Weak chains of reasoning.
1          1-2 marks                                           1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                   Limited analysis
           Basic definition only.                              Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                             0 marks
           No knowledge.                                       No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 A contestable market is one where the threat of entry, rather than the actual number of firms,
 disciplines incumbents' behaviour (Baumol, 1982).
 In a perfectly contestable market, even a monopolist would price at average cost and earn only
 normal profits to deter hit-and-run entry.
 Distinct from perfect competition – contestable markets can have few firms, but entry and exit must
 be costless.

 AO3 (conditions)
 Low barriers to entry: no legal, technical or strategic barriers preventing new firms from entering.
 Low barriers to exit: no sunk costs so firms can leave without penalty, enabling "hit-and-run"
 entry strategies.
 Equal access to technology and resources: incumbents cannot exploit superior production
 methods to deter entrants.
 Perfect information: potential entrants must know prices, costs and profits.
 Consumer ability to switch: consumers must be willing and able to switch to new entrants for the
 threat to be credible.
 These conditions make incumbents behave as though actually faced with competition, setting
 prices close to AC and avoiding X-inefficiency.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                      6
 2 2 Evaluate whether digital streaming services operate as genuinely contestable markets. [20]

                       AO1                                   AO3                                  AO4
Band                 6 marks                              6 marks                            8 marks
3        5-6 marks                            5-6 marks                          6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge                  Excellent analysis                 Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of streaming     Clear reasoning on whether each    Well-developed two-sided answer
         market characteristics (content      contestability condition is met.   with reasoned judgment.
         production costs, network effects,
         switching behaviour).
2        3-4 marks                            3-4 marks                          3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                       Good analysis                      Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                      Good argument.                     Strong two-sided.
1        1-2 marks                            1-2 marks                          1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                    Limited analysis                   Limited evaluation
         Few points.                          Superficial.                       Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                              0 marks                            0 marks
         No knowledge.                        No analysis.                       No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (For contestability)
 Low marginal cost of delivering streaming – once content is produced, adding subscribers is
 cheap, supporting entry.
 Cloud-based infrastructure (AWS, Azure) reduces upfront capital costs for new entrants.
 Consumers can easily switch or subscribe to multiple services with no long-term contracts.
 Recent entrants (Apple TV+, Disney+, Paramount+, Peacock) have successfully entered and
 gained market share – evidence of contestability.
 Price competition (free/ad-supported tiers, crackdown on password sharing) shows incumbents
 behaving as if disciplined by entry threats.
 Evolving customer base – easier for a new entrant to grab share during market expansion.

 AO1/AO4 (Against contestability)
 Large sunk costs in content production: Netflix spends ~$17bn/year on content. This creates
 significant barriers to entry and exit.
 Brand recognition and network effects favour incumbents (Netflix first-mover advantage).
 Exclusive IP and licensing deals lock out competitors (Disney pulled Marvel/Star Wars content from
 Netflix).
 Data advantages: incumbents have accumulated huge viewership data enabling better
 recommendations and content investment decisions.
 Multi-sided platform economics and bundling (Apple One, Amazon Prime) leverage other business
 lines, disadvantaging standalone entrants.
 Consumer inertia and churn-reduction strategies (password crackdowns, multi-year exclusives)
 reduce switching.
 Despite entry, industry concentration remains high with 4–5 dominant global players.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                   7
 Qualifiers
 The streaming market has features of both contestable and oligopolistic markets.
 Geographic markets differ – US and UK have many entrants; developing markets often dominated
 by one or two.
 Sunk costs in content make it "less than perfectly" contestable but not a pure monopoly.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                8
 Section B.
 3. Pound sterling falls to 12-month low against the US dollar
 3 1 Using a diagram, explain the factors that determine the exchange rate of the pound in a floating
 exchange rate system. [10]

                                 AO1                                                  AO3
Band                           6 marks                                              4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                          3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                                Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of currency demand/supply      Strong chains linking determinants (interest rates,
           and a well-labelled D/S diagram. Wide range of     inflation, growth, trade, speculation) to shifts in D
           determinants.                                      and S.
2          3-4 marks                                          1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                     Limited analysis
           Good knowledge but diagram may contain minor       Weak chains.
           errors.
1          1-2 marks                                          1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                  Limited analysis
           Basic. Missing/wrong diagram.                      Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                            0 marks
           No knowledge.                                      No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 A floating exchange rate is determined by the forces of demand for and supply of the currency in
 foreign exchange markets.
 A D/S diagram with GBP price (in USD) on the Y-axis and quantity of GBP on X-axis. Equilibrium
 where D=S.
 Demand for GBP comes from: foreign buyers of UK exports, foreign investors (FDI, portfolio),
 foreign savers seeking higher UK interest rates, tourism to UK, speculators.
 Supply of GBP comes from: UK importers, UK investors abroad, UK tourists abroad, speculators
 selling GBP.

 AO3 (determinants)
 Relative interest rates: higher UK rates → capital inflows → rising D for GBP → appreciation (hot
 money flows).
 Relative inflation rates: higher UK inflation → UK goods less competitive → falling D for UK
 exports → depreciation (PPP theory).
 Relative growth rates: stronger UK growth may raise imports (depreciation) but also attract
 investment (appreciation) – net effect ambiguous.
 Current account: persistent deficits increase GBP supply on forex markets, creating downward
 pressure.
 Speculation: currency traders buy/sell based on expected future moves.
 Political stability: uncertainty (e.g. Brexit, political instability) can cause capital flight.
 Central bank interventions: buying/selling reserves to influence the rate.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                      9
 3 2 Evaluate the impact of a sustained depreciation of the pound on the UK economy. [20]

                       AO1                               AO3                               AO4
Band                 6 marks                          6 marks                         8 marks
3        5-6 marks                        5-6 marks                       6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge              Excellent analysis              Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of           Excellent reasoning including   Well-developed two-sided answer,
         depreciation effects on trade,   Marshall-Lerner condition and   reasoned judgment.
         inflation, growth, employment.   J-curve.
2        3-4 marks                        3-4 marks                       3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                   Good analysis                   Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                  Good argument.                  Strong two-sided.
1        1-2 marks                        1-2 marks                       1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                Limited analysis                Limited evaluation
         Few points.                      Superficial.                    Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                          0 marks                         0 marks
         No knowledge.                    No analysis.                    No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (Positive effects)
 Exports become cheaper in foreign currency, imports become more expensive domestically.
 SPICED – Strong Pound Imports Cheap Exports Dear; depreciation is the reverse.
 If the Marshall-Lerner condition holds (|PEDx| + |PEDm| > 1), depreciation improves the current
 account in the long run (J-curve).
 Export-led growth: higher net exports boost AD, real GDP, and employment.
 Inward tourism and foreign investment can increase as UK assets become cheaper in USD.
 Relief for industries competing with imports – import substitution.

 AO1/AO4 (Negative effects)
 Imported inflation: higher prices for oil, gas, raw materials and consumer goods, squeezing real
 incomes (particularly salient during UK cost-of-living crisis).
 UK firms dependent on imported inputs face higher costs, squeezing profit margins.
 Short run: J-curve effect – current account deteriorates before improving as trade volumes take
 time to respond.
 May trigger Bank of England interest rate hikes to contain inflation, which could hurt growth and
 investment.
 Debt servicing costs rise for UK firms/government with foreign-currency-denominated debt.
 Reduces purchasing power of UK tourists and expats abroad.
 Signals weakness in the UK economy, damaging investor confidence.

 Qualifiers
 Depends on the Marshall-Lerner condition holding.
 Depends on the cause of depreciation (export boom vs capital flight).
 Depends on global economic conditions (trading partners in recession limit export gains).


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                         10
 Depends on the degree of import dependency of UK firms.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                           11
 4. Chancellor announces £30bn increase in public spending despite rising national debt
 4 1 Explain the difference between discretionary fiscal policy and automatic stabilisers. [10]

                                 AO1                                                AO3
Band                           6 marks                                            4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                         3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                               Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of both concepts with clear   Clear chains linking how each operates to stabilise
           examples.                                         the economy.
2          3-4 marks                                         1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                    Limited analysis
           Concepts understood, less detail.                 Weak chains.
1          1-2 marks                                         1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                 Limited analysis
           Basic.                                            Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                           0 marks
           No knowledge.                                     No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 Fiscal policy is the use of government spending (G) and taxation (T) to influence aggregate
 demand and the economy.
 Discretionary fiscal policy: deliberate changes to G or T by government decision (e.g. Chancellor
 announcing £30bn increased spending, VAT cut during Covid, stimulus packages).
 Automatic stabilisers: features of the tax and benefit system that automatically dampen
 economic cycles without explicit government action.
 Examples of automatic stabilisers: progressive income tax (takes more in boom, less in recession),
 unemployment benefits (rise when economy weakens), corporation tax receipts (fall in recession).

 AO3
 Automatic stabilisers operate without recognition/decision/implementation lags, responding
 immediately to economic conditions.
 Discretionary policy suffers from time lags – recognition, decision, implementation – meaning it
 may be pro-cyclical when it arrives.
 In a recession: tax revenues fall automatically as incomes/profits decline, benefits rise
 automatically as unemployment rises → AD supported without explicit action.
 Discretionary policy can be targeted (e.g. stamp duty holiday for housing, furlough scheme for
 workers).
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                               12
 4 2 Evaluate the extent to which expansionary fiscal policy is effective in stimulating economic
 growth. [20]

                       AO1                                   AO3                                  AO4
Band                 6 marks                               6 marks                           8 marks
3        5-6 marks                            5-6 marks                          6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge                  Excellent analysis                 Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of               Strong chains of reasoning         Well-developed two-sided,
         expansionary fiscal policy and its   including the multiplier effect.   considers crowding out, debt
         transmission.                                                           sustainability, Ricardian
                                                                                 equivalence. Reasoned judgment.
2        3-4 marks                            3-4 marks                          3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                       Good analysis                      Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                      Good argument.                     Strong two-sided.
1        1-2 marks                            1-2 marks                          1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                    Limited analysis                   Limited evaluation
         Few points.                          Superficial.                       Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                              0 marks                            0 marks
         No knowledge.                        No analysis.                       No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (Effective)
 Higher G or lower T raises AD (AD = C + I + G + X – M), shifting AD rightward and increasing real
 GDP in the short run (Keynesian AD/AS).
 The multiplier effect means each £1 of initial spending generates more than £1 of final output as
 recipients spend their income (multiplier = 1/MPS+MPT+MPM).
 Government investment in infrastructure (HS2, road building, broadband) raises LRAS as well as
 AD, providing supply-side benefits.
 Education and training spending raises human capital and productivity.
 Countercyclical spending during recessions prevents hysteresis (long-term unemployment
 destroying human capital).
 Can crowd in private investment by improving expectations and reducing risk.
 During recessions, fiscal multipliers are larger because spare capacity exists and monetary policy
 may be at the zero lower bound.

 AO1/AO4 (Limitations)
 Crowding out: higher government borrowing can push up interest rates, reducing private
 investment – the public-sector multiplier is offset by private-sector contraction.
 Ricardian equivalence: households may anticipate future tax rises needed to repay debt and
 save more today, offsetting the stimulus.
 Debt sustainability: UK national debt is over 100% of GDP. Market concerns could trigger bond
 yield spikes (Truss mini-budget 2022).
 Time lags: discretionary fiscal policy takes time to design and implement.
 Inflationary risks: if the economy is near full capacity, extra spending causes inflation rather than
 real growth.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                               13
 Currency depreciation: higher borrowing may weaken the currency, raising imported inflation.
 Leakages to imports: in an open economy, some stimulus leaks out as imports.
 Political capture: spending may be directed to marginal constituencies rather than productive
 uses.
 Alternative policies: monetary policy (if rates aren't at ZLB), supply-side reforms, macroprudential
 tools.

 Qualifiers
 Depends on state of the economy – effective in recession, less so at full capacity.
 Depends on the type of spending (investment vs consumption; productive vs consumption).
 Depends on state of public finances and debt credibility.
 Depends on openness of the economy and the fiscal multiplier.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                    14
 Section C.
 5. UK current account deficit widens to a record £71bn
 5 1 With the aid of a diagram or balance, explain the main components of the balance of payments.
 [10]

                                 AO1                                                  AO3
Band                           6 marks                                              4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                            3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                                  Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of the current account (goods,   Strong chains linking components together;
           services, primary and secondary income), capital     understanding that BoP must balance overall.
           and financial accounts. Accurate diagram/table
           showing balance.
2          3-4 marks                                            1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                       Limited analysis
           Main components identified with some errors in       Weak chains.
           sub-components.
1          1-2 marks                                            1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                    Limited analysis
           Basic understanding only.                            Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                              0 marks
           No knowledge.                                        No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 The balance of payments records all financial transactions between UK residents and the rest of
 the world over a period.
 Current account: trade in goods (merchandise); trade in services (financial, tourism, education);
 primary income (interest, profits, dividends); secondary income (transfers, aid, remittances, EU
 contributions).
 Capital account: transfers of capital such as debt forgiveness, patents and copyrights (small in
 size).
 Financial account: FDI, portfolio investment (shares, bonds), reserve assets, other investment.
 In theory the BoP sums to zero – a current account deficit is offset by a capital and financial
 account surplus.

 AO3
 The UK runs a persistent current account deficit (historically driven by trade in goods), but partially
 offset by a services surplus (financial/insurance services, education).
 Primary income can be positive or negative depending on net ownership of foreign assets and
 earnings thereon.
 A current account deficit must be financed – by foreign purchases of UK assets (FDI, gilts,
 equities), or by running down reserves.
 If foreign confidence declines, financing the deficit becomes harder – potentially triggering currency
 depreciation.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                               15
 5 2 Assess the extent to which a persistent current account deficit is a problem for the UK. [20]

                       AO1                                  AO3                                  AO4
Band                 6 marks                             6 marks                            8 marks
3        5-6 marks                           5-6 marks                          6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge                 Excellent analysis                 Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of effects of   Strong reasoning on implications   Balanced answer considering
         current account deficits.           for exchange rates, debt,          sustainability, composition,
                                             competitiveness.                   circumstances. Reasoned
                                                                                judgment.
2        3-4 marks                           3-4 marks                          3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                      Good analysis                      Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                     Good argument.                     Strong two-sided.
1        1-2 marks                           1-2 marks                          1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                   Limited analysis                   Limited evaluation
         Few points.                         Superficial.                       Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                             0 marks                            0 marks
         No knowledge.                       No analysis.                       No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (Yes, it is a problem)
 A persistent deficit implies that the UK is consuming more than it produces, living beyond its means
 – sustainable only while foreigners lend.
 Deficit signals structural uncompetitiveness of UK goods (slow productivity growth, high unit labour
 costs).
 Financing requires foreign ownership of UK assets – over time, profits, rents and interest flow
 abroad, reducing future primary income.
 Risk of sudden stop: if international investors lose confidence, capital flight triggers currency crisis
 and forced adjustment (e.g. ERM crisis 1992, Turkey, Argentina).
 May force higher interest rates to attract capital inflows, damaging investment and growth.
 Deindustrialisation – loss of manufacturing capacity and associated jobs.
 Exchange rate depreciation imports inflation.

 AO1/AO4 (No, not necessarily a problem)
 The UK has run a current account deficit for most of the past 40 years without triggering crisis –
 sterling is a reserve currency, gilts are in demand globally.
 If the deficit finances productive investment (FDI into UK factories), it builds capacity and future
 export potential.
 Consumption deficits may reflect strong UK growth relative to trading partners – demand-led rather
 than structural.
 Floating exchange rates provide an adjustment mechanism – persistent deficits trigger
 depreciation, eventually improving competitiveness.
 BoP constraints are weaker for countries with reserve currencies and deep capital markets (US,
 UK, Japan).


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                               16
 Services surplus (especially City of London financial services, education, creative industries)
 partially offsets goods deficit.
 The twin deficits hypothesis suggests the CA deficit mirrors fiscal deficit – addressing one
 addresses the other.

 Qualifiers
 Depends on the size relative to GDP (generally <4% sustainable).
 Depends on how the deficit is financed (FDI vs short-term portfolio flows).
 Depends on the cause (cyclical growth vs structural uncompetitiveness).
 Depends on the currency – reserve currency countries have much more flexibility.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                               17
 6. FDI into Africa hits record $83bn as investors target energy transition projects
 6 1 Explain how foreign direct investment (FDI) can contribute to economic development in
 low-income countries. [10]

                                 AO1                                               AO3
Band                           6 marks                                           4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                        3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                              Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of FDI and its channels of   Strong chains linking FDI to employment, tax
           development impact.                              revenue, technology transfer, infrastructure.
2          3-4 marks                                        1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                   Limited analysis
           Narrower range of channels.                      Weak chains.
1          1-2 marks                                        1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                Limited analysis
           Basic.                                           Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                          0 marks
           No knowledge.                                    No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 FDI is long-term cross-border investment by a firm in productive assets in another country, typically
 giving control or significant influence (≥10% ownership).
 Forms: greenfield investment (new facilities), brownfield (expansion/acquisition), mergers and
 acquisitions, joint ventures.

 AO3 (channels of development impact)
 Fills the savings-investment gap: injection of capital raises investment, which through
 Harrod-Domar raises growth.
 Employment creation: direct jobs in MNC facilities, indirect jobs via the multiplier effect and in
 supply chains.
 Tax revenue: corporation tax, income tax, VAT – enlarging the government's fiscal capacity to fund
 health, education, infrastructure.
 Technology and knowledge transfer: training of local workforce, spillovers to domestic firms
 through supplier/customer linkages.
 Balance of payments: FDI inflows (financial account surplus) help finance current account deficits
 and build foreign exchange reserves.
 Infrastructure development: host-country infrastructure often developed to support MNC operations
 (ports, roads, power).
 Access to global markets: MNCs integrate local producers into global value chains.
 Competition effects: pressure domestic firms to raise productivity.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                            18
 6 2 Evaluate whether FDI is always beneficial for host developing countries. [20]

                      AO1                               AO3                                   AO4
Band                6 marks                          6 marks                             8 marks
3        5-6 marks                       5-6 marks                           6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge             Excellent analysis                  Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of both     Strong reasoning including profit   Reasoned judgment considering
         sides.                          repatriation, transfer pricing,     context, regulation, sector.
                                         resource curse.
2        3-4 marks                       3-4 marks                           3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                  Good analysis                       Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                 Good argument.                      Strong two-sided.
1        1-2 marks                       1-2 marks                           1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge               Limited analysis                    Limited evaluation
         Few points.                     Superficial.                        Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                         0 marks                             0 marks
         No knowledge.                   No analysis.                        No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (For)
 All the development channels from Part 1 – capital, employment, technology, infrastructure, tax
 revenue, integration into world economy.
 East Asian miracle economies (Korea, Taiwan, China) used FDI as a key component of
 development strategy.
 FDI into renewable energy in Africa supports the green transition, building sustainable capacity.
 Higher-quality jobs with MNC wages often above local averages, raising living standards.

 AO1/AO4 (Against / costs)
 Profit repatriation: over time, profits flow back to MNC home countries, worsening the primary
 income balance and creating a long-run drain.
 Transfer pricing: MNCs manipulate intra-firm prices to shift profits to low-tax jurisdictions,
 reducing host-country tax revenue. (Starbucks, Google, Apple cases).
 Enclave economies: extractive FDI (oil, mining) creates isolated enclaves with few linkages to the
 wider economy.
 Race to the bottom: countries compete with tax breaks, relaxed labour and environmental
 regulation to attract FDI – undermining development.
 Resource curse: FDI in extractive industries creates currency appreciation, Dutch disease, and
 rent-seeking rather than broad-based development.
 Displacement of local firms: superior MNC scale and technology crowds out infant domestic
 industries.
 Cultural erosion and loss of sovereignty: MNCs bring standardised global culture and reduce
 government autonomy.
 Labour abuses: some MNCs exploit weak labour regulation (Rana Plaza, Bangladesh).
 Volatile capital flows: portfolio FDI can reverse rapidly in crises.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                             19
 Qualifiers
 Depends on the sector – manufacturing/renewables generally more beneficial than extractive
 industries.
 Depends on host-country governance and regulatory capacity.
 Depends on the form of FDI (greenfield vs M&A;).
 Depends on complementary policies (education, infrastructure, tax administration).
 Fully credit all other relevant points.
